revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会家忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「神道…」

revision.text:
『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。
『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。
== 倭姫命伝説形成の経緯 == 『倭姫命世記』の伝説は、[[平安時代]]末期以降、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[御厨]]と呼ばれる[[荘園]]が各地に広がったことや、[[室町時代]]以降に御師と呼ばれる下級神職が各地で伊勢信仰を広めたことと関係していると考えられている<ref>[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。[[倭姫命]]の伝説は広く浸透しており、倭姫命が滞在したという宮の伝承地が、[[岐阜県]]や[[滋賀県]]、[[三重県]]の各地に点在している<ref>[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
* http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_b.html

『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。
『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。
== 倭姫命伝説形成の経緯 == 『倭姫命世記』の伝説は、[[平安時代]]末期以降、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[御厨]]と呼ばれる[[荘園]]が各地に広がったことや、[[室町時代]]以降に御師と呼ばれる下級神職が各地で伊勢信仰を広めたことと関係していると考えられている<ref>[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。[[倭姫命]]の伝説は広く浸透しており、倭姫命が滞在したという宮の伝承地が、[[岐阜県]]や[[滋賀県]]、[[三重県]]の各地に点在している<ref>[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。

『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref name="#1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref name="#1"/>。

revision.text:
『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。
『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref name="#1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref name="#1"/>。
== 成立 == 『倭姫命世記』の伝説は、[[平安時代]]末期以降、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[御厨]]と呼ばれる[[荘園]]が各地に広がったことや、[[室町時代]]以降に御師と呼ばれる下級神職が各地で伊勢信仰を広めたことと関係していると考えられている<ref name="#2">[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。『倭姫命世記』を含む、中世に成立したいわゆる「[[神道五部書]]」の中でも、この『倭姫命世記』は『宝基本紀』とともに比較的早く成立したものと考えられており、成立順としては『宝基本紀』に次ぐ2番目に早い成立であると推定され<ref name="伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁">伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁</ref>、『宝基本紀』の思想性をさらに発展させたものとして考えられる<ref name="大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁">大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁</ref>。神護景曇2年（769年）の「禰宜五月麻呂」による編述とする奥書は仮託であるものの、[[御巫清直]]の研究によって、同書は、神宮の古記録である『太神宮本紀』を基にした『[[大同 (日本)|大同]]本紀』という古代の文献をもとに製作されたものであることが明らかとなり、『倭姫命世記』は、単に中世に編述されたというだけでなく、'''成立に当たっては神宮の古伝承も包摂された'''と考えられている<ref name="大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁">大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁</ref>。
[[倭姫命]]の伝説は広く浸透しており、倭姫命が滞在したという宮の伝承地が、[[岐阜県]]や[[滋賀県]]、[[三重県]]の各地に点在している<ref name="#2"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_b.html {{DEFAULTSORT:やまとひめのみことせいき}} [[Category:鎌倉時代の書籍]] [[Category:古代史]]

『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。
『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref name="#1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref name="#1"/>。
== 成立 == 『倭姫命世記』の伝説は、[[平安時代]]末期以降、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[御厨]]と呼ばれる[[荘園]]が各地に広がったことや、[[室町時代]]以降に御師と呼ばれる下級神職が各地で伊勢信仰を広めたことと関係していると考えられている<ref name="#2">[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。『倭姫命世記』を含む、中世に成立したいわゆる「[[神道五部書]]」の中でも、この『倭姫命世記』は『宝基本紀』とともに比較的早く成立したものと考えられており、成立順としては『宝基本紀』に次ぐ2番目に早い成立であると推定され<ref name="伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁">伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁</ref>、『宝基本紀』の思想性をさらに発展させたものとして考えられる<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁">大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁</ref>。神護景曇2年（769年）の「禰宜五月麻呂」による編述とする奥書は仮託であるものの、[[御巫清直]]の研究によって、同書は、神宮の古記録である『太神宮本紀』を基にした『[[大同 (日本)|大同]]本紀』という古代の文献をもとに製作されたものであることが明らかとなり、『倭姫命世記』は、単に中世に編述されたというだけでなく、'''成立に当たっては神宮の古伝承も包摂された'''と考えられている<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。
[[倭姫命]]の伝説は広く浸透しており、倭姫命が滞在したという宮の伝承地が、[[岐阜県]]や[[滋賀県]]、[[三重県]]の各地に点在している<ref name="#2"/>。
== 研究史 == 「神道五部書」に対する実証的な研究は、[[江戸時代]]中期の国学者[[吉見幸和]]が[[元文]]元年（1736年）に著した『五部書説弁』により端緒が開かれ、幸和は同書で、『倭姫命世記』が五部書の中で最も遅い成立であると推定し<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>、中世の外宮神官による偽作であるとして全面的に批判した。しかし、江戸時代後期の国学者[[本居宣長]]は、『倭姫命世記』の本文に神宮の古伝承が含まれていることを指摘し、さらに[[幕末]]から[[明治時代]]の国学者である[[御巫清直]]は『太神宮本紀帰正抄』を著して、『倭姫命世記』が『太神宮本紀』を基にした『大同本紀』に依拠して成立したものであることを論証し、『倭姫命世記』本文の徹底的な批判検証により元の文章の復元を図って研究を大成した<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。戦後に入ると、[[岡田米男]]、[[西田長男]]、[[久保田収]]らの研究者によりさらに精密な研究が行われ、『倭姫命世記』から直接に『太神宮本紀』の文章を復元することの誤りや、『倭姫命世記』が依拠した神宮の古伝承の範囲などが明らかになっていった<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_b.html {{DEFAULTSORT:やまとひめのみことせいき}} [[Category:鎌倉時代の書籍]] [[Category:古代史]]

『'''倭姫命世記'''』（やまとひめのみことせいき）は、[[鎌倉時代]]中期に編纂されたと考えられている書物。
『倭姫命世記』内では[[神護景雲]]2年（[[768年]]）、「禰宜五月麻呂」の撰と伝えるが、実際は[[鎌倉時代]]中期の[[度会行忠]]により編纂されたと考えられている<ref name="#1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457 コトバンク]</ref>。「[[神道五部書]]」の一つで、『[[記紀]]』で[[垂仁天皇]]の皇女とされる[[倭姫命]]が[[天照大神]]を奉じて各地を巡幸し伊勢に鎮座するまでの伝承を記す<ref>[https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html]『倭姫命世記』「貴重和本デジタルライブラリー」</ref><ref name="#1"/>。
== 成立 == 『倭姫命世記』の伝説は、[[平安時代]]末期以降、[[伊勢神宮]]の[[御厨]]と呼ばれる[[荘園]]が各地に広がったことや、[[室町時代]]以降に御師と呼ばれる下級神職が各地で伊勢信仰を広めたことと関係していると考えられている<ref name="#2">[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23]斎宮歴史博物館「第23話 罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』」</ref>。『倭姫命世記』を含む、中世に成立したいわゆる「[[神道五部書]]」の中でも、この『倭姫命世記』は『宝基本紀』とともに比較的早く成立したものと考えられており、成立順としては『宝基本紀』に次ぐ2番目に早い成立であると推定され<ref name="伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁">伊藤聡『神道とは何か』中公新書(2012),99頁</ref>、『宝基本紀』の思想性をさらに発展させたものとして考えられる<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁">大隅和雄『中世神道論』岩波書店(1977),368頁</ref>。神護景曇2年（769年）の「禰宜五月麻呂」による編述とする奥書は仮託であるものの、[[御巫清直]]の研究によって、同書は、神宮の古記録である『太神宮本紀』を基にした『[[大同 (日本)|大同]]本紀』という古代の文献をもとに製作されたものであることが明らかとなり、『倭姫命世記』は、単に中世に編述されたというだけでなく、'''成立に当たっては神宮の古伝承も包摂された'''と考えられている<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。
[[倭姫命]]の伝説は広く浸透しており、倭姫命が滞在したという宮の伝承地が、[[岐阜県]]や[[滋賀県]]、[[三重県]]の各地に点在している<ref name="#2"/>。
== 研究史 == 「神道五部書」に対する実証的な研究は、[[江戸時代]]中期の国学者[[吉見幸和]]が[[元文]]元年（1736年）に著した『五部書説弁』により端緒が開かれ、幸和は同書で、『倭姫命世記』が五部書の中で最も遅い成立であると推定し<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>、中世の外宮神官による偽作であるとして全面的に批判した。しかし、江戸時代後期の国学者[[本居宣長]]は、『倭姫命世記』の本文に神宮の古伝承が含まれていることを指摘し、さらに[[幕末]]から[[明治時代]]の国学者である[[御巫清直]]は『太神宮本紀帰正抄』を著して、『倭姫命世記』が『太神宮本紀』を基にした『大同本紀』に依拠して成立したものであることを論証し、『倭姫命世記』本文の徹底的な批判検証により元の文章の復元を図って研究を大成した<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。戦後に入ると、[[岡田米男]]、[[西田長男]]、[[久保田収]]らの研究者によりさらに精密な研究が行われ、『倭姫命世記』から直接に『太神宮本紀』の文章を復元することの誤りや、『倭姫命世記』が依拠した神宮の古伝承の範囲などが明らかになっていった<ref name="大隅、1977、368頁"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_b.html {{DEFAULTSORT:やまとひめのみことせいき}} [[Category:鎌倉時代の書籍]] [[Category:古代史]]

revision.text: {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki|en}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Chronicle of Princess Yamatohime]] to [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki]]

revision.text:
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[Kamakura period]].
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of [[Ise Shinto]], the [[Shinto Gobusho]]. The work records the traditions of [[Yamatohime-no-Mikoto]], identified in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[Amaterasu]] before finally enshrining her at [[Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of Ise Shinto.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition == The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[Ise Shrine]]’s [[manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as [[Kubota Osamu]] have attributed its compilation to [[Watarai Yukitada]], though [[Kamada Junichi]] suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''Hōki Honki,'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by [[Mikannagi Kiyonao]] have revealed that the text was based on ''Daijingū Honki,'' an ancient document of [[Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the [[Senmyō-gata]] style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of [[Yamatohime-no-Mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[Gifu Prefecture]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], and [[Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine == ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes Shinto philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of [[Kōtai Jingu]] by Yamatohime-no-Mikoto.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the [[Shikinen Sengu]].<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
A passage from the divine oracle, "The gods grant blessings through prayer; the unseen bestows prosperity through honesty," influenced later Shinto thought, including the [[Suiren Shintō]] founded by [[Yamazaki Ansai]].<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107"/>
== Research History == Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar [[Yoshimi Yukikazu]] in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by [[Outer Shrine]] priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient Ise traditions in the text. Further analysis by [[Mikannagi Kiyonao]] demonstrated its basis in ''Daijingū Honki.'' Postwar research by scholars like [[Okada Yoneo]], [[Nishida Osamu]], and [[Kubota Osamu]] further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others=[[Kazuo Ōsumi]] (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others=[[Koremaru Sakamoto]] & [[Kenji Ishii]] (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author=Satoshi Itō |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author=Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Yamato-hime no Mikoto Seiki}} [[Category:Kamakura period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:New pages]][[category:original pages]][[ja:%E5%80%AD%E5%A7%AB%E5%91%BD%E4%B8%96%E8%A8%98]]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

{{Medieval Shinto}} {{Shinto Sidebar}}
[[Category:Kamakura-period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[ja:倭姫命世記]]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

[[Category:Kamakura-period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[Category:dardar]] [[category:most recent 500]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:倭姫命世記]]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

[[Category:Kamakura-period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:倭姫命世記]]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō-gata|ja|宣命体}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

[[Category:Kamakura-period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:倭姫命世記]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}

[[Category:Kamakura-period texts]] [[Category:Ancient history]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Yamatohime-no-mikoto Seiki]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:倭姫命世記]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki}}

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}
