However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>
== Composition ==
The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}’s {{ill|manors|ja|荘園}} (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and the spread of Ise faith by lower-ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}.<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}},'' an ancient document of {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>
Legends of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|ja|岐阜県}}, {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|ja|滋賀県}}, and {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="#2"/>
== Doctrine ==
''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref>
Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>
== Research History ==
Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|外宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}} recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>
== References == * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Kazuo Ōsumi|ja|大隅和雄}} (ed.) |title=Chūsei Shintō-ron |publisher=Iwanami Shoten |series=Nihon Shisō Taikei 19 |date=1977 |isbn=978-4-00-070019-1}} * {{Cite book |others={{ill|Koremaru Sakamoto|ja|阪本是丸}} & {{ill|Kenji Ishii|ja|石井研士}} (eds.) |title=Prestep Shintōgaku |publisher=Kōbundō |date=2011 |isbn=978-4-335-00079-9}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Satoshi Itō|ja|伊藤聡}} |title=Shintō to wa nani ka |publisher=Chūō Kōron Shinsha |series=Chūkō Shinsho 2158 |date=2012 |isbn=978-4-12-102158-8}} * {{Cite book |author={{ill|Kaifu Yawotome|ja|海部やをとめ}} & {{ill|Mitsuhashi Ken|ja|三橋健}} |title=Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari |publisher=Fuyōsha International |date=2018}}
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_b.html Official Site]

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.
Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of {{ill|Jingokeiun|ja|神護景雲}} (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-{{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}.<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}, the {{ill|Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書}}. The work records the traditions of {{ill|Yamatohime-no-Mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}, identified in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' as the daughter of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}, journeying across various regions with the deity {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} before finally enshrining her at {{ill|Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|qq=|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|qq=|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|qq=|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|qq=|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|qq=|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|qq=|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|qq=|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭姫命世記]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q131331326]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:倭姫命世記]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q131331326]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki''' (倭姫命世記) is a text believed to have been compiled in the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].

Within ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki,'' it is stated that the work was composed in the second year of [[:en:Jingo-keiun|Jingokeiun]] (768) by "Nenge Mayumaro." However, it is widely believed to have been compiled during the mid-[[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]].<ref name="#1">{{Cite web|title=倭姫命世記|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/倭姫命世記-649457|publisher=コトバンク}}</ref> It is one of the five foundational texts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shintō Gobusho|lt=Shinto Gobusho|ja|神道五部書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto Gobusho|qq=}}. The work records the traditions of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], identified in the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'' and ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' as the daughter of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], journeying across various regions with the deity [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] before finally enshrining her at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/54.html|title=倭姫命世記|publisher=愛知県立図書館デジタルライブラリー}}</ref><ref name="#1"/> The text also strongly asserts the doctrines of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}}.<ref name="Osumi1977p367">[[Kazuo Ōsumi]], ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'' (中世神道論), Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 367.</ref>

== Composition ==

The legends of ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' are closely related to the expansion of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]]’s [[:en:Manorialism|manors]] (shōen) called ''Mikuriya'' throughout various regions from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and the spread of Ise faith by lower-Ranking priests called ''Oshi'' after the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="#2">{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/saiku/hyakuwa/journal.asp?record=23|title=罪作りだよ？『倭姫命世紀』|publisher=斎宮歴史博物館}}</ref> Scholars such as {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} have attributed its compilation to {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}}, though {{ill|Kamada Junichi|ja|鎌田純一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamada Junichi|1=Jun'ichi Kamata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11651081}} suggested that it had already been composed before Yukitada’s time.<ref name="Ban1993p5-6">Ban Gojirō, ''Kaidai,'' ''Shintō Taikei: Ise Shintō (Vol. 1)'', Shintō Taikei Hensankai, 1993, pp. 5–6.</ref> Among the medieval "Shinto Gobusho," ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' is thought to have been composed relatively early, following ''{{ill|Hōki Honki|ja|宝基本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' and further developing the philosophical ideas of that text.<ref name="Itō2012p99">[[:en:Satoshi Itō|Satoshi Itō]], ''Shintō to wa nani ka'' (神道とは何か), Chūō Kōron Shinsha, 2012, p. 99.</ref><ref name="Osumi1977p368">Kazuo Ōsumi, ''Chūsei Shintō-ron'', Iwanami Shoten, 1977, p. 368.</ref> While the attribution to Nenge Mayumaro in Jingokeiun 2 is fictional, studies by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} have revealed that the text was based on ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}},'' an ancient document of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Shrine]], and incorporated traditional legends of the shrine.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

Its literary style combines elements of the {{ill|Senmyō|ja|宣命体|Senmyō=Senmyō|lt=Senmyō-gata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senmyō|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=宣命}} style but is not entirely consistent. The latter sections are predominantly written in a pure [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] style with few annotations, blending classical and Kamakura-period language.<ref name="Nakamura2012p441">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū: Fukkun to Dokkai,'' Shintensha, 2012, p. 441.</ref><ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3">Nakamura Yukihiro, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki Kenkyū,'' pp. 2–3.</ref> The use of classical Senmyō style is considered an archaic embellishment to give the impression of antiquity.<ref name="Nakamura2012p2-3"/>

Legends of [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] are widely recognized, and places said to have been her temporary palaces are scattered across [[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]], and [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="#2"/>

== Doctrine ==

''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' emphasizes [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] philosophy alongside the narrative of the enshrinement of {{ill|Kōtai Jingu|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="Osumi1977p367"/> The text records that shortly before her passing in the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], Yamatohime-no-Mikoto delivered a divine oracle. It describes the human heart as a divine spirit, or "Shinjin," emphasizing that humans must live in a state of "Seichoku" (正直, honesty) and "Seijō" (清浄, purity).<ref name="KaifuMitsuhashi2018p105-107">Kaifu Yawotome & Mitsuhashi Ken, ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto-sama no Monogatari,'' Fuyōsha International, 2018, pp. 105–107.</ref> 

Another oracle, "Return to the origin, and align yourself with the fundamental essence," conveys the idea of renewal through recognizing and restoring one’s connection to the source, a concept known as ''Motomoto Honhon.'' This philosophy resonates with the rituals of the {{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|式年遷宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=遷宮}}.<ref name="SakamotoIshii2011p55">Sakamoto Koremaru & Ishii Kenji, ''Prestep Shintōgaku,'' Kōbundō, 2011, p. 55.</ref>

== Research History ==

Empirical research on the ''Shinto Gobusho'' began with the Edo-period scholar {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}} in ''Gobusho Setsuben'' (1736). Yukikazu criticized ''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Seiki'' as the latest among the ''Gobusho,'' claiming it was a medieval fabrication by {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=Kōtai Jingū|lt=Outer Shrine|ja|外宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=豊受大神宮}} priests.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/> However, later scholars such as [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] recognized the inclusion of ancient {{ill|Ise traditions|ja|伊勢伝承|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise traditions|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the text. Further analysis by {{ill|Mikannagi Kiyonao|ja|御巫清直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi Kiyonao|1=Mikannagi Kiyonao|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6537624}} demonstrated its basis in ''{{ill|Daijingū Honki|ja|太神宮本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijingū Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.'' Postwar research by scholars like {{ill|Okada Yoneo|ja|岡田米男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okada Yoneo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nishida Osamu|ja|西田長男|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishida Osamu|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kubota Osamu|ja|久保田収|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kubota Osamu|1=Osamu Kubota|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11369065}} further refined understanding of the text’s origins and its incorporation of ancient traditions.<ref name="Osumi1977p368"/>

== References ==
